title: Local hooks and server checks

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Local hooks and server checks; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.claim: Git hooks run local or server-side programs at lifecycle points to automate checks, but their installation, environment, trust, and bypass policy must be explicit.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Git hooks run local or server-side programs at lifecycle points to automate checks, but their installation, environment, trust, and bypass policy must be explicit.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A versioned hook launcher runs fast formatting checks before commit while authoritative CI reruns all required checks independently. Your goal is to design a check placement plan for local, pre-push and CI stages. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Dependencies and reproducible builds; Commits and history; Formatter and linter; Build and task commands. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Git hooks run local or server-side programs at lifecycle points to automate checks, but their installation, environment, trust, and bypass policy must be explicit. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Client hooks are not cloned automatically, can be bypassed, execute with developer privileges, and may behave differently across shells and platforms. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: A pre-commit hook guarantees every commit in the remote passed the check and is safe to trust.

includes: Use fast local feedback as convenience while keeping authoritative checks reproducible outside developer hooks.
Design a check placement plan for local, pre-push and CI stages.
excludes: Client hooks are not cloned automatically, can be bypassed, execute with developer privileges, and may behave differently across shells and platforms.

outcomes: Design a check placement plan for local, pre-push and CI stages.

examples.title: Local hooks and server checks: direct case
examples.situation: A versioned hook launcher runs fast formatting checks before commit while authoritative CI reruns all required checks independently.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A versioned hook launcher runs fast formatting checks before commit while authoritative CI reruns all required checks independently.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Treat hooks as convenience and defense in depth, distribute them transparently, minimize privilege, and enforce non-bypassable policy on trusted CI or server controls.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Treat hooks as convenience and defense in depth, distribute them transparently, minimize privilege, and enforce non-bypassable policy on trusted CI or server controls.

examples.title: Local hooks and server checks: boundary case
examples.situation: A downloaded repository's hook scripts are reviewed before installation because they can execute arbitrary local commands.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A downloaded repository's hook scripts are reviewed before installation because they can execute arbitrary local commands.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Client hooks are not cloned automatically, can be bypassed, execute with developer privileges, and may behave differently across shells and platforms.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: A pre-commit hook guarantees every commit in the remote passed the check and is safe to trust.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design a check placement plan for local, pre-push and CI stages.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A pre-commit hook guarantees every commit in the remote passed the check and is safe to trust.
misconceptions.correction: Treat hooks as convenience and defense in depth, distribute them transparently, minimize privilege, and enforce non-bypassable policy on trusted CI or server controls.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: A pre-commit hook guarantees every commit in the remote passed the check and is safe to trust.

analogy.example: A controlled investigation can model Local hooks and server checks: preserve evidence, state a hypothesis, inspect one clue, automate repeatable checks, and narrow the change point.
usefulFor: Linking debugging, static checks, history search, and automated local gates
limitations: Debuggers and hooks can alter timing or execute code, and tool findings remain bounded evidence rather than proof.

recap.summary: You should now be able to design a check placement plan for local, pre-push and CI stages. Keep this boundary in view: Client hooks are not cloned automatically, can be bypassed, execute with developer privileges, and may behave differently across shells and platforms.
